subtitle: Thursday, 18 June 2026
editor_note: This week's digest looks at how governments and AI companies are navigating an increasingly tense relationship, with several stories tracing a dispute between Anthropic (maker of the Claude AI models) and the US government. We also turn to South Korea, where strong public enthusiasm and new investment are shaping a national AI ecosystem, and consider what AI means for the value of human expertise and skills. Together, these stories highlight themes close to our mandate: workforce development, public-sector AI adoption, and the policies that guide responsible use.

items.title: Anthropic Pulls Claude Fable and Mythos AI Models After Feds Claim Jailbreak - CNET
items.summary: Anthropic withdrew two of its AI models, named Claude Fable and Mythos, after US federal authorities said the systems could be tricked into bypassing their safety controls (a tactic known as 'jailbreaking'). The company pulled the models while it reviews the concerns.
items.why_it_matters: It shows that even leading AI tools can have safety gaps, underlining the need for careful vetting before adopting AI in public services.

items.title: Why do South Koreans love AI so much?
items.summary: South Korea has embraced AI in everyday life, from automated immigration checkpoints to widespread use on public transport and in daily services. The article explores the cultural and policy reasons behind this enthusiasm and high adoption.
items.why_it_matters: It offers a real-world example of how a society and government can integrate AI into public services, relevant as the UAE pursues similar national ambitions.

items.title: Agentic coding and persistent returns to expertise - Anthropic
items.summary: Anthropic published research on 'agentic coding,' where AI assistants carry out programming tasks more independently. The findings suggest that experienced, skilled workers still gain the most from these tools, as expertise remains valuable even as AI takes on more work.
items.why_it_matters: It reinforces that investing in skills and training keeps people valuable in an AI-enabled workplace, a core part of our human-capital mission.

items.title: Anthropic opens Seoul office and announces new partnerships across the Korean AI ecosystem - Anthropic
items.summary: Anthropic opened a new office in Seoul and announced partnerships across South Korea's AI sector. The move is part of the company's effort to expand its presence and collaborations in Asia.
items.why_it_matters: It illustrates how AI firms grow through local partnerships and offices, a model worth watching as the Gulf builds its own AI ecosystem.

items.title: Anthropic Meeting With Trump Admin As Feud Grows Deeper - Forbes
items.summary: Anthropic met with the Trump administration amid a deepening disagreement between the company and the US government. Reports describe ongoing friction over how the company's AI models are handled and regulated.
items.why_it_matters: It shows the growing tension between AI companies and governments over oversight, a dynamic that shapes global AI policy and standards.

items.title: Anthropic Is Still at Odds With the White House Over Claude Fable 5 - WIRED
items.summary: Anthropic remains in dispute with the White House over its Claude Fable 5 model, continuing the disagreement covered in related stories this week. The two sides have not resolved their differences over the model.
items.why_it_matters: It highlights how unresolved questions about AI safety and government oversight can affect which tools become available and trusted for official use.

items.text: ● DRAFT — for Policy & Strategy review
AI Pulse — Weekly AI News Digest
Thursday, 18 June 2026

body.text: This week's digest looks at how governments and AI companies are navigating an increasingly tense relationship, with several stories tracing a dispute between Anthropic (maker of the Claude AI models) and the US government. We also turn to South Korea, where strong public enthusiasm and new investment are shaping a national AI ecosystem, and consider what AI means for the value of human expertise and skills. Together, these stories highlight themes close to our mandate: workforce development, public-sector AI adoption, and the policies that guide responsible use.

items.text: [1. Anthropic Pulls Claude Fable and Mythos AI Models After Feds Claim Jailbreak - CNET](https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMingFBVV95cUxPS0t6Wng3RzNPalVucXJsTWptTnhsNDF0VUI4M3ZjWTVjRUk5eTJNMmZvNE9aMjBRdXV6dWxfWFhVMkFEakhKM3dfTjFWY29VeG4zX3N4SkhhcTUtMnlpeVc4WlVvWmxVZnZyZEE5MHlqVmN1OFVZekRraGJKYUhkUkQtUnlaZVFRTjQ4ODZMa3poMDVVcjBJTXRJLTY2Zw?oc=5)

Anthropic withdrew two of its AI models, named Claude Fable and Mythos, after US federal authorities said the systems could be tricked into bypassing their safety controls (a tactic known as 'jailbreaking'). The company pulled the models while it reviews the concerns.
**Why it matters for Tamkeen:** It shows that even leading AI tools can have safety gaps, underlining the need for careful vetting before adopting AI in public services.
Anthropic (via Google News) · 16 Jun 2026
[2. Why do South Koreans love AI so much?](https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/06/15/1138983/why-do-south-koreans-love-ai-so-much/)

South Korea has embraced AI in everyday life, from automated immigration checkpoints to widespread use on public transport and in daily services. The article explores the cultural and policy reasons behind this enthusiasm and high adoption.
**Why it matters for Tamkeen:** It offers a real-world example of how a society and government can integrate AI into public services, relevant as the UAE pursues similar national ambitions.
MIT Technology Review — AI · 15 Jun 2026

[3. Agentic coding and persistent returns to expertise - Anthropic](https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiZ0FVX3lxTE9lM0p2cGtZWWJ4aE9Ocy05NEU1SXFraWl1V1gwNmxwdGpMWkg3N2Z3WGF6eFFMZXlWMGZEeGZZVGxmcGJyVlBSLXlCUXhma0lWRm1SNllTSVlmNXlTMFEzLTZIMFIwSDA?oc=5)

Anthropic published research on 'agentic coding,' where AI assistants carry out programming tasks more independently. The findings suggest that experienced, skilled workers still gain the most from these tools, as expertise remains valuable even as AI takes on more work.
**Why it matters for Tamkeen:** It reinforces that investing in skills and training keeps people valuable in an AI-enabled workplace, a core part of our human-capital mission.
Anthropic (via Google News) · 16 Jun 2026

[4. Anthropic opens Seoul office and announces new partnerships across the Korean AI ecosystem - Anthropic](https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiggFBVV95cUxQcHh0bjBqVXNvaGk0ZVVsQkJZd2RhMUpQYU00SkxDUS1VQXdORkg5cjdaOHZQNlExR2R3Y1I0Mnpwbm1vR2lLU1doa1BmQ0NYbEY2YXJ4SC1BTGdWVWhnZGJ5dUhKSWFIRlNaVWpFa011RHFkUWdBNzgzRVUzU3Nnc1lB?oc=5)
Anthropic opened a new office in Seoul and announced partnerships across South Korea's AI sector. The move is part of the company's effort to expand its presence and collaborations in Asia.
**Why it matters for Tamkeen:** It illustrates how AI firms grow through local partnerships and offices, a model worth watching as the Gulf builds its own AI ecosystem.
Anthropic (via Google News) · 17 Jun 2026

[5. Anthropic Meeting With Trump Admin As Feud Grows Deeper - Forbes](https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiywFBVV95cUxPWDgxb25zbU9OUVEwUXJYNFBZZUl5MkpvUGk0TTNxdFg4WUg0aDljcWNrMGtCd1NMam9xWTB1TmNhOU1mOHQ3R3RRVnlxVl81MzlSVEdIU2NhYUE4QUxDc3U3Y01idmFESXh5X0d4RTkxMFpkeFljQTNJVnEtbkRxWDNncXMyeU5nbTY2NGxCUlIxS3BHZ0pFWG9KOWlKdGsxbXhZV1hqU1RSdW10aDJOMWtGcnRaSmxwa2F4aF83ekZURDVsRmFsdTNxaw?oc=5)

Anthropic met with the Trump administration amid a deepening disagreement between the company and the US government. Reports describe ongoing friction over how the company's AI models are handled and regulated.
**Why it matters for Tamkeen:** It shows the growing tension between AI companies and governments over oversight, a dynamic that shapes global AI policy and standards.
Anthropic (via Google News) · 15 Jun 2026

[6. Anthropic Is Still at Odds With the White House Over Claude Fable 5 - WIRED](https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMinAFBVV95cUxQWG9ZWjBhNGVHb2xPbURHZ0NNa1hHV2ZDN2FXNUZEak54VnV2YWtrSHd1SWhmZUtEaUZuZ0NzaGgyNTQ4c3B2OE9UNTJkcGdSMEVjWGZGckU5ckFDbm9sQ1JTeUVyVzZ1MVNzSEJKbWxKWEU1QlBPMEpVVWY4b2dYV1VPVzN4aThoMWdHMXUtV29CNUZmVm5wWUpVRXY?oc=5)

Anthropic remains in dispute with the White House over its Claude Fable 5 model, continuing the disagreement covered in related stories this week. The two sides have not resolved their differences over the model.
**Why it matters for Tamkeen:** It highlights how unresolved questions about AI safety and government oversight can affect which tools become available and trusted for official use.
Anthropic (via Google News) · 16 Jun 2026

plain_text:
*** DRAFT — for Policy & Strategy review ***

AI Pulse — Weekly AI News Digest
Thursday, 18 June 2026
============================================================
This week's digest looks at how governments and AI companies are navigating an increasingly tense relationship, with several stories tracing a dispute between Anthropic (maker of the Claude AI models) and the US government. We also turn to South Korea, where strong public enthusiasm and new investment are shaping a national AI ecosystem, and consider what AI means for the value of human expertise and skills. Together, these stories highlight themes close to our mandate: workforce development, public-sector AI adoption, and the policies that guide responsible use.

1. Anthropic Pulls Claude Fable and Mythos AI Models After Feds Claim Jailbreak - CNET
   https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMingFBVV95cUxPS0t6Wng3RzNPalVucXJsTWptTnhsNDF0VUI4M3ZjWTVjRUk5eTJNMmZvNE9aMjBRdXV6dWxfWFhVMkFEakhKM3dfTjFWY29VeG4zX3N4SkhhcTUtMnlpeVc4WlVvWmxVZnZyZEE5MHlqVmN1OFVZekRraGJKYUhkUkQtUnlaZVFRTjQ4ODZMa3poMDVVcjBJTXRJLTY2Zw?oc=5
   Anthropic withdrew two of its AI models, named Claude Fable and Mythos, after US federal authorities said the systems could be tricked into bypassing their safety controls (a tactic known as 'jailbreaking'). The company pulled the models while it reviews the concerns.
   Why it matters for Tamkeen: It shows that even leading AI tools can have safety gaps, underlining the need for careful vetting before adopting AI in public services.
   (Anthropic (via Google News) · 16 Jun 2026)

2. Why do South Koreans love AI so much?
   https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/06/15/1138983/why-do-south-koreans-love-ai-so-much/
   South Korea has embraced AI in everyday life, from automated immigration checkpoints to widespread use on public transport and in daily services. The article explores the cultural and policy reasons behind this enthusiasm and high adoption.
   Why it matters for Tamkeen: It offers a real-world example of how a society and government can integrate AI into public services, relevant as the UAE pursues similar national ambitions.
   (MIT Technology Review — AI · 15 Jun 2026)

3. Agentic coding and persistent returns to expertise - Anthropic
   https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiZ0FVX3lxTE9lM0p2cGtZWWJ4aE9Ocy05NEU1SXFraWl1V1gwNmxwdGpMWkg3N2Z3WGF6eFFMZXlWMGZEeGZZVGxmcGJyVlBSLXlCUXhma0lWRm1SNllTSVlmNXlTMFEzLTZIMFIwSDA?oc=5
   Anthropic published research on 'agentic coding,' where AI assistants carry out programming tasks more independently. The findings suggest that experienced, skilled workers still gain the most from these tools, as expertise remains valuable even as AI takes on more work.
   Why it matters for Tamkeen: It reinforces that investing in skills and training keeps people valuable in an AI-enabled workplace, a core part of our human-capital mission.
   (Anthropic (via Google News) · 16 Jun 2026)

4. Anthropic opens Seoul office and announces new partnerships across the Korean AI ecosystem - Anthropic
   https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiggFBVV95cUxQcHh0bjBqVXNvaGk0ZVVsQkJZd2RhMUpQYU00SkxDUS1VQXdORkg5cjdaOHZQNlExR2R3Y1I0Mnpwbm1vR2lLU1doa1BmQ0NYbEY2YXJ4SC1BTGdWVWhnZGJ5dUhKSWFIRlNaVWpFa011RHFkUWdBNzgzRVUzU3Nnc1lB?oc=5
   Anthropic opened a new office in Seoul and announced partnerships across South Korea's AI sector. The move is part of the company's effort to expand its presence and collaborations in Asia.
   Why it matters for Tamkeen: It illustrates how AI firms grow through local partnerships and offices, a model worth watching as the Gulf builds its own AI ecosystem.
   (Anthropic (via Google News) · 17 Jun 2026)

5. Anthropic Meeting With Trump Admin As Feud Grows Deeper - Forbes
   https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiywFBVV95cUxPWDgxb25zbU9OUVEwUXJYNFBZZUl5MkpvUGk0TTNxdFg4WUg0aDljcWNrMGtCd1NMam9xWTB1TmNhOU1mOHQ3R3RRVnlxVl81MzlSVEdIU2NhYUE4QUxDc3U3Y01idmFESXh5X0d4RTkxMFpkeFljQTNJVnEtbkRxWDNncXMyeU5nbTY2NGxCUlIxS3BHZ0pFWG9KOWlKdGsxbXhZV1hqU1RSdW10aDJOMWtGcnRaSmxwa2F4aF83ekZURDVsRmFsdTNxaw?oc=5
   Anthropic met with the Trump administration amid a deepening disagreement between the company and the US government. Reports describe ongoing friction over how the company's AI models are handled and regulated.
   Why it matters for Tamkeen: It shows the growing tension between AI companies and governments over oversight, a dynamic that shapes global AI policy and standards.
   (Anthropic (via Google News) · 15 Jun 2026)

6. Anthropic Is Still at Odds With the White House Over Claude Fable 5 - WIRED
   https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMinAFBVV95cUxQWG9ZWjBhNGVHb2xPbURHZ0NNa1hHV2ZDN2FXNUZEak54VnV2YWtrSHd1SWhmZUtEaUZuZ0NzaGgyNTQ4c3B2OE9UNTJkcGdSMEVjWGZGckU5ckFDbm9sQ1JTeUVyVzZ1MVNzSEJKbWxKWEU1QlBPMEpVVWY4b2dYV1VPVzN4aThoMWdHMXUtV29CNUZmVm5wWUpVRXY?oc=5
   Anthropic remains in dispute with the White House over its Claude Fable 5 model, continuing the disagreement covered in related stories this week. The two sides have not resolved their differences over the model.
   Why it matters for Tamkeen: It highlights how unresolved questions about AI safety and government oversight can affect which tools become available and trusted for official use.
   (Anthropic (via Google News) · 16 Jun 2026)
